content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
t (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
1/ 
Teresa L. Highsmith 
Colantuono, Highsmith, Whatley, PC 
300 S. Grand Avenue, Suite 2700 
Los Angeles, CA 90071 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-150 
Dear Ms. Highsmith: 
December 19, 2016 
You are the General Counsel of the South East Los Angeles County Work Force 
Development Policy Board ("Policy Board"). This letter responds to your request for advice on 
behalf of Policy Board member Fernando Vazquez regarding applicable conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1and Section 1090. We offer no opinion on the 
application of other laws, which may apply, such as common law conflict-of-interest laws or 
restrictions on incompatible activities. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair 
Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (/11 re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
As required by Section 1097. l(c)(S), we have forwarded your request to both the Attorney 
General's Office and the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office. We have not received a 
response from either office. Finally, we are required to advise you that Section 1090 advice issued 
by the Commission applies only to proceedings brought by the Commission, and the advice is not 
admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (See Section 
1097.1 (c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
As a member of the Policy Board, does Fernando Vasquez have a conflict of interest in 
requesting services and potentially entering into a contract with the South East Los Angeles County 
Workforce Development Board (''Workforce Development Board") when the Policy Board 
appoints the members of the Workforce Development Board, approves its budget, and approves its 
higher level contracts? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-16-150 
Page No. 2 
Policy Board Member Vasquez is not prohibited from contracting with the Workforce 
Development Board under Section 1090, however he is prohibited under the Act from making, 
participating in, or using his official position to influence the decision. Accordingly, while his 
company may enter into the contract, any appearances or communications with the Workforce 
Development Board must be made by a representative of the company and not Policy Board 
Member Vazquez. Additionally, documents submitted to the Workforce Development Board should 
not reference Policy Board Member Vazquez except as required under procedures available to any 
other contracting party. 
FACTS 
Workforce Development Board Composition and Powers 
The Workforce Development Board is California Nonprofit Public Benefit corporation 
formed to perform the duties of a "local workforce investment board" as defined by the federal 
Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act of 2014 ("WIOA''). The Workforce Development 
Board was originally formed under the federal Workforce Investment Act of 1998, which has now 
been superseded by WIOA. The Workforce Development Board is governed by a 24-member 
Board of Directors, the majority of whom are representatives of local businesses. The Workforce 
Development Board, implementing the provisions ofWIOA, provides services to job seekers and 
employers, includingjob training for the Southeast Los Angeles County (e.g., the cities of Artesia, 
Bellflower, Cerritos, Downey, Hawaiian Gardens, Lakewood and Norwalk). 
These types of services include, but are not limited to: 
• 
Providing outreach to job seekers currently enrolled in the Workforce Development Board 
American Job Center for direct hiring. 
• 
Connecting persons seeking to get off of welfare with job-training and work experience through 
local participating businesses willing to provide the on-site employment opportunity (the 
Workforce Development Board provides the hourly pay for the participants, workers 
compensation costs and support services). 
The Workforce Development Board is the grant recipient of the State of California's local 
area share ofWIOA funds and acts as the "fiscal agent" with respect to these funds. The Workforce 
Development Board is empowered to make decisions regarding the distribution of services to 
participants and disbursement of WIOA grant funds to pay for those services and entering into 
contracts for these purposes. The Workforce Development Board is staffed by the Executive 
Director, who regularly makes recommendations to the Workforce Development Board regarding 
the provision of such services. Additionally, the Executive Director of the Workforce Development 
Board hires the Program Managers who will administer the various contracts entered into by the 
Workforce Development Board and the program participants. A request by a business to contract 
with the Workforce Development Board for the services offered by the Workforce Development 
Board is not made directly to the Workforce Development Board. The process includes filing an 
application with Workforce Development Board staff who implement the programs within the 


File No. A-16-150 
Page No. 3 
budget and funding approved by the Workforce Development Board. There are no in-person 
appearances before the Workforce Development Board by requestors for these contracts. 
A. Policy Board Composition and Powers 
The Policy Board, is a Joint Powers Authority comprised of the cities of Artesia, Bellflower, 
Cerritos, Downey, Hawaiian Gardens, Lakewood and Norwalk. The Policy Board is governed by a 
seven-member board, comprised of one elected official appointed by each of the seven participating 
cities. Board Member Fernando Vasquez was appointed to the Policy Board by the City Council of 
the City of Downey and is also City of Downey Mayor Pro Tern. The purpose of the Policy Board 
is to perfonn the role of the "Chief Elected Official" pursuant to the WIOA. As such, the Policy 
Board has the authority to appoint members to the Workforce Development Board, approve the 
Workforce Development Board annual budget for all programs, services, revenues and expenditures 
and review the monthly and quarterly reports from the Workforce Development Board regarding 
program perfonnance. 
The Policy Board also has approval authority for certain "major contracts," defined as a 
lease of any space for longer than five years, a contract for purposes other than delivery of services 
obligating the Workforce Development Board to pay more than $100,000 within a 12-month period, 
approval of the selection of the Executive Director to the Workforce Development Board and any 
settlement of a formal legal proceeding. The Executive Director is employed by the Workforce 
Development Board and provides staffing support to the Workforce Development Board. As stated 
previously, the Executive Director makes recommendations to the Workforce Development Board 
regarding the provision of services to participants and these recommendations are regularly 
followed by the Workforce Development Board. The Policy Board does not approve, review or 
oversee the individual service contracts for disbursement ofWIOA grant funds to program 
participants (this is the purview of the Workforce Development Board). 
B. Board Member Vasquez' Financial Interest 
Policy Board Member Vasquez is a managing partner and owns two-thirds of Downey & 
2nd, LLC, a limited liability corporation OBA Mi Cielo ("Mi Cielo"). Gerardo Verdugo owns one­
third of Mi Cielo and is also a managing partner. Mi Cielo is a restaurant. The restaurant has the 
potential to employ up to 60 employees. As a business owner, Policy Board Member Vasquez is 
interested in accessing the services currently available through the Workforce Development Board 
workforce system. The services he may wish to access will require a contractual arrangement 
between Mi Cielo and the Workforce Development Board. As noted above, the request for the 
contractual arrangement is not made directly to the Workforce Development Board. It is an 
application made to Workforce Development staff, who implement the programs within the budget 
and funding approved by the Workforce Development Board. The services include: 
• 
Outreach to job seekers currently enrolled in the Workforce Development Board American Job 
Center for direct hiring. 
• 
Accessing resources that cover paid work experience for WIOA participants by offering the 
restaurant environment to serve as a work site. The workforce system will provide hourly pay to 
the participant, cover workers' compensation fees, and any required support services such as 


File No. A-16-150 
Page No. 4 
uniforms to ensure a successful work experience assignment. Time frame for all work 
experience opportunities range from 80 hours to 500 hours based on the funding available to 
support the participant. (The Workforce Development Board is the employer of record, not the 
restaurant, in this context. The restaurant serves as the training site for work experience and 
receives the employment services offered by the employees during this on-the-job training.) 
• 
Accessing on-the-job training support by using the workforce system to recruit potential 
candidates. In situations in which participants appear to be good candidates but are lacking 
specific skills, Mr. Vasquez is willing to commit the restaurant's management staff to provide 
on-the-job training for the program participants. A specific time frame is determined for 
learning the skills and as long as the skills are acquired and the program participants meet all 
training expectations they are maintained on the job as a full-time employee. In return for 
supporting the development and training of the new hire, Workforce Development Board 
commits to use workforce funds to support a portion of the hourly wage of the trainee during the 
duration of the agreed training period. In this context, the restaurant is the employer of record. 
D. Request for Advice 
Policy Board Member Vasquez requests written advice as to whether he or his restaurant 
may enter into a contract with the Workforce Development Board to receive services and grant 
funding through WIOA, as disbursed by the Workforce Development Board, given the following 
facts: 
1. The Policy Board would not be involved in the approval or oversight of such an agreement, 
and therefore, Policy Board Member Vasquez would not be taking an action in his official 
capacity. 
2. The Policy Board approves the employment agreement of the Executive Director, whose 
recommendations regarding provision of services to participants (such as potential 
employees of Mr. Vasquez' restaurant) are regularly accepted and approved by the 
Workforce Development Board. 
3. The Executive Director hires the Program Managers who will administer the service 
contracts to program participants. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 covers contracts made by public officials, providing in part: 
"Members of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, and city 
officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by 
them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members. 
Nor shall state, county, district, judicial district, and city officers or employees be 
purchasers at any sale or vendors at any purchase made by them in their official 
capacity." 


File No. A-16-150 
Page No. 5 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers from being financially interested in 
contracts made by them in their official capacity or made by boards or commissions of which they 
are members. (87 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 23, 24 (2004).) The purpose .. is to remove or limit the 
possibility of any personal influence, either directly or indirectly, which might bear upon an 
official's decision, as well as to void contracts which are actually obtained through fraud or 
dishonest conduct." (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) The statutory goal is .. not 
only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of 
Imperial Beach v. Bailey ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 19 I, 197.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is 
void and unenforceable. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) Importantly, Section 1090's 
prohibition applies regardless of whether the contract would otherwise be fair and equitable (id. at 
pp. 646-649), and a board member with a proscribed interest may not avoid the prohibition by 
abstaining from the decision-making process; the entire board is prohibited from executing the 
agreement if the prohibition is applicable. (Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte 
(1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201, 211-212.) 
Under Section I 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest," and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might 
profit from it in any way. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) Although Section 
1090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General 
opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve 
financial losses, or the possibility oflosses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (Thomson, 
supra, at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; Teny 
v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; People v. Darby (1952) 114 Cal.App.2d 412, 431-
432; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).) 
Section 1091.2 provides the following with respect to local workforce investment boards: 
"Section 1090 shall not apply to any contract or grant made by local workforce 
investment boards created pursuant to the federal Workforce Investment Act of 
1998 except where both of the following conditions are met: 
"(a) The contract or grant directly relates to services to be provided by any 
member of a local workforce investment board or the entity the member 
represents or financially benefits the member or the entity he or she represents. 
"(b) The member fails to recuse himself or herself from making, participating in 
making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence a 
decision on the grant or grants." 
Section 1091.2 clearly states that Section 1090 does not apply to any contract made by a 
workforce investment board created pursuant to the federal Workforce Investment Act of 1998, 
except when a member of that board is seeking services from the board and fails to recuse himself 
from its decisions. The Workforce Development Board is a local workforce investment board 
created under the Workforce Investment Act of 1998. In the present circumstances Policy Board 
Member Velazquez is not a member of the Workforce Development Board. Therefore, pursuant to 
Section 1091.2 section 1090 does not apply to the contract at issue in this request. 


The Act 
File No. A-16-150 
Page No. 6 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, 
or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. 
Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "makes a governmental decision" when 
the official, ''official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits 
his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his 
or her agency." (Section 87100; Regulation 18704(a).) 
Participating in Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "participates in a 
governmental decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the 
purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive review." (Section 
87100; Regulation 18704(b).) 
Influencing a Governmental Decision: When the governmental decision is within or before 
the public official's own agency or an agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or 
her agency, if the official "contacts or appears before" or otherwise attempts to influence, any 
member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency, then he or she is attempting to influence a 
governmental decision: (Regulation 18704( c )( 1 ). )2 
In this case because the Workforce Development Board is subject to the budgetary control 
of the Policy Board, Policy Board Member Velazquez is contacting or appearing before his own 
agency when seeking a contract with the Workforce Development Board. Accordingly, Policy 
Board Member Velazquez may not attempt to influence any member, officer, employee, or 
consultant of the Workforce Development Board ifhe has a conflict of interest in the decision. 
A public official has a financial interest, and disqualifying conflict of interest, in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a 
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests. 
(Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. 
These interests are: 
I. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth two 
thousand dollars ($ 2,000) or more. (Section 87103( a).) 
2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand 
dollars ($ 2,000) or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
2 The second rule, which is not applicable here, applies when the governmental decision is within or before an 
agency other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the 
public official's agency. (Regulation 18704(c)(2).) Under this rule, the official cannot act or purport "to act within his or 
her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact" to influence a decision that will have a material 
financial effect on his or her interests. 


File No. A-16-150 
Page No. 7 
3. Any source ofincome, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating five hundred dollars ($ 500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the 
public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, 
or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
5. Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating [$ 460] or 
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to 
the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).) 
Financial Interest 
Policy Board Member Vasquez has an interest in his restaurant business that is worth $2,000 
or more. 
Foreseeability 
Under the Act, a financial effect on a financial interest is presumed foreseeable if the 
interest is explicitly involved in the decision, and an interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is 
a named party in, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) Policy Board Member Vasquez 
and Mi Cielo would be the named party in or subject of the decision in this case because under the 
facts provided they would be applying for a contract with the Workforce Development Board. 
Materiality 
Regulation 18702.1 (a)(6) sets forth the applicable test for materiality and provides that the 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a business entity in which an 
official has a financial interest is material whenever the business entity is the subject of any 
inspection, action, or proceeding subject to the regulatory authority of the official's agency. 
Policy Board Member Vasquez is financially interested in his restaurant business entity, and 
the Workforce Development Board staff will be deciding whether or not to enter into a contract 
with his business. Therefore, his business entity is the subject of the proceeding and the decision 
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Policy Board Member Vasquez's 
financial interest. Accordingly, Policy Board member Vasquez is disqualified from taking part in 
the decision under the Act and may not contact or appear before the Workforce Development Board 
to influence the contract. However, the Act only prohibits contacts or appearances by Policy Board 
Member Vasquez. The Act does not preclude a representative of the company from contacting or 
appearing on behalf of the company. In this case, the co-owner of Mi Cielo, Gerardo Verdugo, may 
represent the restaurant before the Workforce Development Board. (See Strand Advice Letter, No. 
A-01-129.) 
We do, however, caution that Policy Board Member Vasquez should not be identified in any 
documents submitted in the contracting process except as required by the Workforce Development 
Board for any other contracting party. While Policy Board Member Vasquez may not contact or 


File No. A-16-150 
Page No. 8 
appear before the Workforce Development Board, we have previously advised that in limited 
circumstances an official may avail himself of the same procedures available to the public. (See 
Miller Advice Letter, No. A-15-229.) 3To the extent that the Workforce Development Board 
generally requires contracting parties to identify the owners of a contracting business, the Act does 
not preclude the restaurant from identifying Policy Board Member Vazquez as required in 
documents submitted in the contracting process. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
-!< 
By: 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
3 Note that while Regulation 18704 (d)(2) provides a limited exception to the conflict of interest prohibition if 
an official is appearing as a member of the general public to represent his or her personal interest in a business entity, 
this exception does not apply to a business entity unless "owned entirely by the official, members of his or her 
immediate family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family; or a business entity over which the 
official, members of his or her immediate family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family solely or 
jointly exercise full direction and control." In this case, the exception does not apply because the restaurant is co-owned 
and may be represented by Mr. Verdugo. 
4 You have not provided any information regarding decisions made by the Policy Board that may affect the 
contract with Policy Board Member Vasquez's restaurant including any decisions by the Policy Board regarding the 
program under which the restaurant may enter the contract. In this regard, we cannot address any past decisions by the 
Policy Board as the Commission does not provide advice involving past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) 
However, Policy Board Member Vasquez should seek further assistance before making, participating in or using his 
official position to influence decisions by the Policy Board that could affect the program that his business is applying 
for.

sections.question:
As a member of the Policy Board, does Fernando Vasquez have a conflict of interest in 
requesting services and potentially entering into a contract with the South East Los Angeles County 
Workforce Development Board (''Workforce Development Board") when the Policy Board 
appoints the members of the Workforce Development Board, approves its budget, and approves its 
higher level contracts?
sections.conclusion:
Policy Board Member Vasquez is not prohibited from contracting with the Workforce 
Development Board under Section 1090, however he is prohibited under the Act from making, 
participating in, or using his official position to influence the decision. Accordingly, while his 
company may enter into the contract, any appearances or communications with the Workforce 
Development Board must be made by a representative of the company and not Policy Board 
Member Vazquez. Additionally, documents submitted to the Workforce Development Board should 
not reference Policy Board Member Vazquez except as required under procedures available to any 
other contracting party.
sections.facts:
Workforce Development Board Composition and Powers 
The Workforce Development Board is California Nonprofit Public Benefit corporation 
formed to perform the duties of a "local workforce investment board" as defined by the federal 
Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act of 2014 ("WIOA''). The Workforce Development 
Board was originally formed under the federal Workforce Investment Act of 1998, which has now 
been superseded by WIOA. The Workforce Development Board is governed by a 24-member 
Board of Directors, the majority of whom are representatives of local businesses. The Workforce 
Development Board, implementing the provisions ofWIOA, provides services to job seekers and 
employers, includingjob training for the Southeast Los Angeles County (e.g., the cities of Artesia, 
Bellflower, Cerritos, Downey, Hawaiian Gardens, Lakewood and Norwalk). 
These types of services include, but are not limited to: 
• 
Providing outreach to job seekers currently enrolled in the Workforce Development Board 
American Job Center for direct hiring. 
• 
Connecting persons seeking to get off of welfare with job-training and work experience through 
local participating businesses willing to provide the on-site employment opportunity (the 
Workforce Development Board provides the hourly pay for the participants, workers 
compensation costs and support services). 
The Workforce Development Board is the grant recipient of the State of California's local 
area share ofWIOA funds and acts as the "fiscal agent" with respect to these funds. The Workforce 
Development Board is empowered to make decisions regarding the distribution of services to 
participants and disbursement of WIOA grant funds to pay for those services and entering into 
contracts for these purposes. The Workforce Development Board is staffed by the Executive 
Director, who regularly makes recommendations to the Workforce Development Board regarding 
the provision of such services. Additionally, the Executive Director of the Workforce Development 
Board hires the Program Managers who will administer the various contracts entered into by the 
Workforce Development Board and the program participants. A request by a business to contract 
with the Workforce Development Board for the services offered by the Workforce Development 
Board is not made directly to the Workforce Development Board. The process includes filing an 
application with Workforce Development Board staff who implement the programs within the  
budget and funding approved by the Workforce Development Board. There are no in-person 
appearances before the Workforce Development Board by requestors for these contracts. 
A. Policy Board Composition and Powers 
The Policy Board, is a Joint Powers Authority comprised of the cities of Artesia, Bellflower, 
Cerritos, Downey, Hawaiian Gardens, Lakewood and Norwalk. The Policy Board is governed by a 
seven-member board, comprised of one elected official appointed by each of the seven participating 
cities. Board Member Fernando Vasquez was appointed to the Policy Board by the City Council of 
the City of Downey and is also City of Downey Mayor Pro Tern. The purpose of the Policy Board 
is to perfonn the role of the "Chief Elected Official" pursuant to the WIOA. As such, the Policy 
Board has the authority to appoint members to the Workforce Development Board, approve the 
Workforce Development Board annual budget for all programs, services, revenues and expenditures 
and review the monthly and quarterly reports from the Workforce Development Board regarding 
program perfonnance. 
The Policy Board also has approval authority for certain "major contracts," defined as a 
lease of any space for longer than five years, a contract for purposes other than delivery of services 
obligating the Workforce Development Board to pay more than $100,000 within a 12-month period, 
approval of the selection of the Executive Director to the Workforce Development Board and any 
settlement of a formal legal proceeding. The Executive Director is employed by the Workforce 
Development Board and provides staffing support to the Workforce Development Board. As stated 
previously, the Executive Director makes recommendations to the Workforce Development Board 
regarding the provision of services to participants and these recommendations are regularly 
followed by the Workforce Development Board. The Policy Board does not approve, review or 
oversee the individual service contracts for disbursement ofWIOA grant funds to program 
participants (this is the purview of the Workforce Development Board). 
B. Board Member Vasquez' Financial Interest 
Policy Board Member Vasquez is a managing partner and owns two-thirds of Downey & 
2nd, LLC, a limited liability corporation OBA Mi Cielo ("Mi Cielo"). Gerardo Verdugo owns one­
third of Mi Cielo and is also a managing partner. Mi Cielo is a restaurant. The restaurant has the 
potential to employ up to 60 employees. As a business owner, Policy Board Member Vasquez is 
interested in accessing the services currently available through the Workforce Development Board 
workforce system. The services he may wish to access will require a contractual arrangement 
between Mi Cielo and the Workforce Development Board. As noted above, the request for the 
contractual arrangement is not made directly to the Workforce Development Board. It is an 
application made to Workforce Development staff, who implement the programs within the budget 
and funding approved by the Workforce Development Board. The services include: 
• 
Outreach to job seekers currently enrolled in the Workforce Development Board American Job 
Center for direct hiring. 
• 
Accessing resources that cover paid work experience for WIOA participants by offering the 
restaurant environment to serve as a work site. The workforce system will provide hourly pay to 
the participant, cover workers' compensation fees, and any required support services such as  
uniforms to ensure a successful work experience assignment. Time frame for all work 
experience opportunities range from 80 hours to 500 hours based on the funding available to 
support the participant. (The Workforce Development Board is the employer of record, not the 
restaurant, in this context. The restaurant serves as the training site for work experience and 
receives the employment services offered by the employees during this on-the-job training.) 
• 
Accessing on-the-job training support by using the workforce system to recruit potential 
candidates. In situations in which participants appear to be good candidates but are lacking 
specific skills, Mr. Vasquez is willing to commit the restaurant's management staff to provide 
on-the-job training for the program participants. A specific time frame is determined for 
learning the skills and as long as the skills are acquired and the program participants meet all 
training expectations they are maintained on the job as a full-time employee. In return for 
supporting the development and training of the new hire, Workforce Development Board 
commits to use workforce funds to support a portion of the hourly wage of the trainee during the 
duration of the agreed training period. In this context, the restaurant is the employer of record. 
D. Request for Advice 
Policy Board Member Vasquez requests written advice as to whether he or his restaurant 
may enter into a contract with the Workforce Development Board to receive services and grant 
funding through WIOA, as disbursed by the Workforce Development Board, given the following 
facts: 
1. The Policy Board would not be involved in the approval or oversight of such an agreement, 
and therefore, Policy Board Member Vasquez would not be taking an action in his official 
capacity. 
2. The Policy Board approves the employment agreement of the Executive Director, whose 
recommendations regarding provision of services to participants (such as potential 
employees of Mr. Vasquez' restaurant) are regularly accepted and approved by the 
Workforce Development Board. 
3. The Executive Director hires the Program Managers who will administer the service 
contracts to program participants.
sections.analysis:
Section 1090 
Section 1090 covers contracts made by public officials, providing in part: 
"Members of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, and city 
officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by 
them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members. 
Nor shall state, county, district, judicial district, and city officers or employees be 
purchasers at any sale or vendors at any purchase made by them in their official 
capacity."  
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers from being financially interested in 
contracts made by them in their official capacity or made by boards or commissions of which they 
are members. (87 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 23, 24 (2004).) The purpose .. is to remove or limit the 
possibility of any personal influence, either directly or indirectly, which might bear upon an 
official's decision, as well as to void contracts which are actually obtained through fraud or 
dishonest conduct." (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) The statutory goal is .. not 
only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of 
Imperial Beach v. Bailey ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 19 I, 197.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is 
void and unenforceable. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) Importantly, Section 1090's 
prohibition applies regardless of whether the contract would otherwise be fair and equitable (id. at 
pp. 646-649), and a board member with a proscribed interest may not avoid the prohibition by 
abstaining from the decision-making process; the entire board is prohibited from executing the 
agreement if the prohibition is applicable. (Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte 
(1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201, 211-212.) 
Under Section I 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest," and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might 
profit from it in any way. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) Although Section 
1090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General 
opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve 
financial losses, or the possibility oflosses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (Thomson, 
supra, at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; Teny 
v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; People v. Darby (1952) 114 Cal.App.2d 412, 431-
432; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).) 
Section 1091.2 provides the following with respect to local workforce investment boards: 
"Section 1090 shall not apply to any contract or grant made by local workforce 
investment boards created pursuant to the federal Workforce Investment Act of 
1998 except where both of the following conditions are met: 
"(a) The contract or grant directly relates to services to be provided by any 
member of a local workforce investment board or the entity the member 
represents or financially benefits the member or the entity he or she represents. 
"(b) The member fails to recuse himself or herself from making, participating in 
making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence a 
decision on the grant or grants." 
Section 1091.2 clearly states that Section 1090 does not apply to any contract made by a 
workforce investment board created pursuant to the federal Workforce Investment Act of 1998, 
except when a member of that board is seeking services from the board and fails to recuse himself 
from its decisions. The Workforce Development Board is a local workforce investment board 
created under the Workforce Investment Act of 1998. In the present circumstances Policy Board 
Member Velazquez is not a member of the Workforce Development Board. Therefore, pursuant to 
Section 1091.2 section 1090 does not apply to the contract at issue in this request. 


The Act  
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, 
or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. 
Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "makes a governmental decision" when 
the official, ''official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits 
his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his 
or her agency." (Section 87100; Regulation 18704(a).) 
Participating in Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "participates in a 
governmental decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the 
purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive review." (Section 
87100; Regulation 18704(b).) 
Influencing a Governmental Decision: When the governmental decision is within or before 
the public official's own agency or an agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or 
her agency, if the official "contacts or appears before" or otherwise attempts to influence, any 
member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency, then he or she is attempting to influence a 
governmental decision: (Regulation 18704( c )( 1 ). )2 
In this case because the Workforce Development Board is subject to the budgetary control 
of the Policy Board, Policy Board Member Velazquez is contacting or appearing before his own 
agency when seeking a contract with the Workforce Development Board. Accordingly, Policy 
Board Member Velazquez may not attempt to influence any member, officer, employee, or 
consultant of the Workforce Development Board ifhe has a conflict of interest in the decision. 
A public official has a financial interest, and disqualifying conflict of interest, in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a 
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests. 
(Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. 
These interests are: 
I. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth two 
thousand dollars ($ 2,000) or more. (Section 87103( a).) 
2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand 
dollars ($ 2,000) or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
2 The second rule, which is not applicable here, applies when the governmental decision is within or before an 
agency other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the 
public official's agency. (Regulation 18704(c)(2).) Under this rule, the official cannot act or purport "to act within his or 
her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact" to influence a decision that will have a material 
financial effect on his or her interests.  
3. Any source ofincome, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating five hundred dollars ($ 500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the 
public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, 
or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
5. Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating [$ 460] or 
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to 
the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).) 
Financial Interest 
Policy Board Member Vasquez has an interest in his restaurant business that is worth $2,000 
or more. 
Foreseeability 
Under the Act, a financial effect on a financial interest is presumed foreseeable if the 
interest is explicitly involved in the decision, and an interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is 
a named party in, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) Policy Board Member Vasquez 
and Mi Cielo would be the named party in or subject of the decision in this case because under the 
facts provided they would be applying for a contract with the Workforce Development Board. 
Materiality 
Regulation 18702.1 (a)(6) sets forth the applicable test for materiality and provides that the 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a business entity in which an 
official has a financial interest is material whenever the business entity is the subject of any 
inspection, action, or proceeding subject to the regulatory authority of the official's agency. 
Policy Board Member Vasquez is financially interested in his restaurant business entity, and 
the Workforce Development Board staff will be deciding whether or not to enter into a contract 
with his business. Therefore, his business entity is the subject of the proceeding and the decision 
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Policy Board Member Vasquez's 
financial interest. Accordingly, Policy Board member Vasquez is disqualified from taking part in 
the decision under the Act and may not contact or appear before the Workforce Development Board 
to influence the contract. However, the Act only prohibits contacts or appearances by Policy Board 
Member Vasquez. The Act does not preclude a representative of the company from contacting or 
appearing on behalf of the company. In this case, the co-owner of Mi Cielo, Gerardo Verdugo, may 
represent the restaurant before the Workforce Development Board. (See Strand Advice Letter, No. 
A-01-129.) 
We do, however, caution that Policy Board Member Vasquez should not be identified in any 
documents submitted in the contracting process except as required by the Workforce Development 
Board for any other contracting party. While Policy Board Member Vasquez may not contact or  
appear before the Workforce Development Board, we have previously advised that in limited 
circumstances an official may avail himself of the same procedures available to the public. (See 
Miller Advice Letter, No. A-15-229.) 3To the extent that the Workforce Development Board 
generally requires contracting parties to identify the owners of a contracting business, the Act does 
not preclude the restaurant from identifying Policy Board Member Vazquez as required in 
documents submitted in the contracting process. 4

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: As a member of the Policy Board, does Fernando Vasquez have a conflict of interest in 
requesting services and potentially entering into a contract with the South East Los Angeles County 
Workforce Development Board (''Workforce Development Board") when the Policy Board 
appoints the members of the Workforce Development Board, approves its budget, and approves its 
higher level contracts?

CONCLUSION: Policy Board Member Vasquez is not prohibited from contracting with the Workforce 
Development Board under Section 1090, however he is prohibited under the Act from making, 
participating in, or using his official position to influence the decision. Accordingly, while his 
company may enter into the contract, any appearances or communications with the Workforce 
Development Board must be made by a representative of the company and not Policy Board 
Member Vazquez. Additionally, documents submitted to the Workforce Development Board should 
not reference Policy Board Member Vazquez except as required under procedures available to any 
other contracting party.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION • 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 t (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 1/ Teresa L. Highsmith Colantuono, Highsmith, Whatley, PC 300 S. Grand Avenue, Suite 2700 Los Angeles, CA 90071 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-150 Dear Ms. Highsmith: December 19, 2016 You are the General Counsel of the South East Los Angeles County Work Force Development Policy Board ("Policy Board"). This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Policy Board member Fernando Vazquez regarding applicable conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1and Section 1090. We offer no opinion on the application of other laws, which may apply, such as common law conflict-of-interest laws or restrictions on incompatible activities. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (/11 re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) As required by Section 1097. l(c)(S), we have forwarded your request to both the Attorney General's Office and the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office. We have not received a response from either office. Finally, we are required to advise you that Section 1090 advice issued by the Commission applies only to proceedings brought by the Commission, and the advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (See Section 1097.1 (c)(5).) QUESTION As a member of the Policy Board, does Fernando Vasquez have a conflict of interest in requesting services and potentially entering into a contract with the South East Los Angeles County Workforce Development Board (''Workforce Development Board") when the Policy Board appoints the members of the Workforce Development Board, approves its budget, and approves its higher level contracts? 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. CONCLUSION File No. A-16-150 Page No. 2 Policy Board Member Vasquez is not prohibited from contracting with the Workforce Development Board under Section 1090, however he is prohibited under the Act from making, participating in, or using his official position to influence the decision. Accordingly, while his company may enter into the contract, any appearances or communications with the Workforce Development Board must be made by a representative of the company and not Policy Board Member Vazquez. Additionally, documents submitted to the Workforce Development Board should not reference Policy Board Member Vazquez except as required under procedures available to any other contracting party. FACTS Workforce Development Board Composition and Powers The Workforce Development Board is California Nonprofit Public Benefit corporation formed to perform the duties of a "local workforce investment board" as
